title: Minutes of the Federal Open Market Committee March 17–18, 2026
summary: The FOMC minutes from March 17-18, 2026 reveal significant market shifts during the intermeeting period driven by Middle East conflict and AI sector concerns. Crude oil front-month futures surged approximately 50 percent, while broad equity prices fell about 5 percent, with the software sector notably underperforming. Market pricing for rate cuts has been substantially pushed back, with a rate cut not fully priced in until December and the probability of rate hikes through early next year rising to approximately 30 percent.
key_points: Middle East conflict drove crude oil front-month futures prices up approximately 50 percent during the intermeeting period
Broad equity prices fell about 5 percent over the period; software sector underperformed and leveraged loan prices for software firms declined sharply
Market-implied probability of rate hikes through early next year increased to about 30 percent; a rate cut is not fully priced in until December
Median Desk survey path still shows two 25 basis point rate cuts this year, but timing pushed out; some respondents shifted toward fewer cuts after survey
Treasury yields ended higher, more so at short end; term premium changes accounted for substantial fraction of yield changes
Several central banks previously expected to remain on hold or ease (ECB, BoC, SNB) are now expected to hike rates modestly this year due to energy-driven inflation
Standing repo operations reached $30 billion on one day, the third-largest volume since inception, suggesting greater willingness to use operations following December changes
Reserves projected to average about $3 trillion through September; monthly pace of reserve management purchases likely to be reduced significantly after April
PCE inflation was 2.8 percent in January (source truncated)
Some private credit funds experienced notable increases in redemption requests through quarterly redemption opportunities
why_it_matters: This FOMC minutes release captures a pivotal market regime shift where energy price shocks from the Middle East conflict and AI sector disruptions have materially altered rate expectations. The divergence between market pricing (no cuts until December) and the Desk survey median (two cuts) suggests uncertainty about the inflation-growth tradeoff. The rise in rate hike probabilities to 30 percent marks a significant repricing from previous expectations, with implications across asset classes. The mention of elevated redemption requests at private credit funds signals potential liquidity stress in that market segment.
macro_context: The source documents a significant shift in the macro outlook driven by supply-side energy shocks from the Middle East conflict. Near-term inflation expectations rose (one-year inflation swap rate up nearly 50 bps), but longer-term inflation compensation was little changed, suggesting markets view the price spike as potentially transitory. The conflict has created divergent expectations across central banks, with ECB, BoC, and SNB now expected to hike rates even as the Fed's path remains uncertain.
market_structure_context: Treasury market liquidity diminished somewhat but continued to function well. Standing repo usage reached $30 billion on one day with high Treasury settlement volumes, suggesting increased willingness to use the facility after December operational changes. Money market conditions remained broadly stable with effective federal funds rate 1 basis point below IORB and repo rates near IORB. Reserves projected to average $3 trillion through September with reserve management purchase pace likely to be reduced after April.
important_numbers: 50 percent (crude oil front-month futures price increase)
50 basis points (one-year inflation swap rate rise)
30 percent (probability of rate hikes through early next year)
notes: Source is a primary Federal Reserve document with high domain trust. The March 17-18, 2026 FOMC minutes capture a market regime transition period. Market structure relevance includes Treasury liquidity monitoring, standing repo facility usage patterns, and private credit redemption dynamics. Macro catalyst relevance is high given energy price shock, inflation expectations repricing, and central bank policy divergence documented. Source is recent relative to TODAY (within 90 days).
